solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public long maximumOr(int[] nums, int k) {
        long[] prefix = new long[nums.length];
        long[] suffix = new long[nums.length];
        long[] multiplied = new long[nums.length];

        for (int i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) {
            prefix[i] = (i == 0 ? 0 : prefix[i - 1]) | nums[i];
            multiplied[i] = (long) nums[i] << k;
        }

        for (int i = nums.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            suffix[i] = (i == nums.length - 1 ? 0 : suffix[i + 1]) | nums[i];
        }

        long maxOr = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) {
            long left = (i == 0 ? 0 : prefix[i - 1]);
            long right = (i == nums.length - 1 ? 0 : suffix[i + 1]);
            long current = (left | multiplied[i]) | right;
            maxOr = Math.max(maxOr, current);
        }

        return maxOr;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses prefix and suffix arrays to efficiently compute the bitwise OR of elements to the left and right of each index. It then simulates applying the multiplication operation to each element at most k times and computes the maximum possible OR value. The approach is efficient with linear time and space complexity.
